coredata.dc:title: Role of carbon quantum dots on Nickel titanate to promote water oxidation reaction under visible light illumination
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Long
Cai, Pingru
Liu, Zuozhen
Xie, Zailai
Fang, Yuanxing
coredata.dc:description: Water oxidation reaction (WOR) is the heart for overall water splitting owing to its sluggish kinetics. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are studied as co-catalyst to promote WOR by loading them on NiTiO3 (NTO) photocatalyst. The performance can be obtained in a fold of 7 compared with pristine NTO in power-based photocatalytic system, and strong stability has received with preserving the output for at least 10 h. The CQDs have also demonstrated to load on NTO based photoanode for WOR, and a 6 times increasement has realized. In-situ characterizations have acquired to study the roles of CQDs for WOR and found that CQDs can facilitate the chemical adsorption of water molecules, and meanwhile promote the formation of hydroxyl radical as transition states of WOR. This demonstration presents a clue to understand the role of carbon in photocatalytic system to promote WOR and encourage its uses for advanced photoredox catalytic reactions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Carbon quantum dots
Water oxidation reaction
Photoelectrochemical system
Nickel titanate

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of pristine NiTiO3 and loading of carbon quantum dots on NiTiO3
Preparation of NTO and CQDs/NTO photoanodes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical (a) SEM and (b) TEM images of C/NTO-4, and the inset of (a) presented the analysis of the diameter of the NTO particles. (c) HR-TEM of C/NTO-4. STEM studies of C/NTO-4 including (d) HAADF image, and the elements of (e) carbon, (f) titanium, (g) nickel and (h) oxygen. (i) FTIR spectra of C/NTO samples.

(a) WOR performances of CQDs loaded NTO powder and illuminated under a 300 W Xe lamp irradiation with a cut-off filter of 420 nm, and (b) the corresponding measurement for 10 h. (c) The transient photocurrent density by 0.142 mg/cm2 CQDs loaded NTO photoanode under illumination of AM 1.5G, and (d) the corresponding measurement for 15 h.
(a) The FTIR spectra to study the chemical adsorption of D2O of surface of samples. (b) i-EPR spectra to show the difference of produced •OH during WOR.

head.ce:title: Role of carbon quantum dots on Nickel titanate to promote water oxidation reaction under visible light illumination

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350002, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water oxidation reaction (WOR) is the heart for overall water splitting owing to its sluggish kinetics. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are studied as co-catalyst to promote WOR by loading them on NiTiO3 (NTO) photocatalyst. The performance can be obtained in a fold of 7 compared with pristine NTO in power-based photocatalytic system, and strong stability has received with preserving the output for at least 10 h. The CQDs have also demonstrated to load on NTO based photoanode for WOR, and a 6 times increasement has realized. In-situ characterizations have acquired to study the roles of CQDs for WOR and found that CQDs can facilitate the chemical adsorption of water molecules, and meanwhile promote the formation of hydroxyl radical as transition states of WOR. This demonstration presents a clue to understand the role of carbon in photocatalytic system to promote WOR and encourage its uses for advanced photoredox catalytic reactions.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxygenic photosynthesis is the most important metabolic innovation in Earth history to originate the oxygen-based lives [1,2]. Photosystem is driven by sunlight to possess the sluggish water oxidation reaction (WOR) for freeing oxygen gas and subsequently accumulation of electrons to produce carbohydrates. Artificial photosynthesis, or namely photocatalysis, can be applied to produce fuels or chemicals by reductive half-reactions, such as reduction of atmospheric CO2 into hydrocarbons, and conversion of water to hydrogen gas, but restricted by a reliable and efficient photocatalyst for WOR [3,4].

To date, the thermodynamic requirements for WOR can be readily satisfied, and the suitable examples include TiO2 [5], Fe2O3 [6], WO3 [7], FeTiO3 [8], and many others [9–12]. In comparison, surface reaction, especially within WOR, is recognized to restrict quantum efficiency and thus hinder its further developments [13]. Co-catalyst, or namely electrocatalyst, is therefore considered as the key component in the photocatalysis system to facilitate WOR [14,15]. Noble metals have been widely studied as the co-catalyst for WOR, including Ir, Ru, Pt and more [16–18]. However, its sophisticated synthesis and scarcity highly limit their further investigations, and practical uses.

In electrocatalysis, carbon materials have trigged intensive interesting for a variety of redox reactions in the manners of cost-effectiveness and conventional synthesis [19–21]. Examples have been witnessed to apply carbon materials for electrocatalytic WOR, implying its potential to take to role of co-catalyst for photocatalytic WOR [22–25]. Recent studies have demonstrated the use of the carbon dots in a homogeneously photocatalytic system to promote surface reactions [26–28]. In addition, examples were also witnessed to improve the photoanodic current density by loading nanostructured carbon species as co-catalyst on TiO2 and BiVO4 photoanodes, and the reaction rate of WOR can be significantly improved [29,30]. On basis of those studies, it is highly desired to understand the roles of carbon on the surface of photocatalysts for WOR.

In this work, NiTiO3 (NTO) is studied as a proof-of-concept photocatalyst since it is an emerging photocatalyst with appropriate thermodynamic properties for WOR, but restricted by kinetics [31–34]. Both powder-based photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical systems have been acquired as platforms to investigate the WOR. By virtue of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) loaded NTO, for the powder based photocatalytic half WOR, the performances are boosted with a fold of 7 to the pristine NTO powder. In addition, we have realized CQDs/NTO photoanodes, and its optimal photocurrent density is ca. 6 times to the pristine one. We have acquired Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) to study chemical adsorption of water molecule on the samples. Moreover, in-situ electron paramagnetic resonance (i-EPR) has acquired to study the transition state of WOR, namely hydroxyl radical (•OH), and the concentration of which has improved when CQDs loads on NTO. This study has presented the roles of CQDs for the boosted WOR and offers opportunities to rational design of carbon materials for advanced applications of photoredox catalysis.

Nickel (II) acetate tetrahydrate [Ni(CH3COO)2·4H2O, 99.9%], titanium (IV) isopropoxide [C12H28O4Ti, 99.9%], niobium chloride [NbCl5, 99.5%], (Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China), anhydrous citric acid [C6H8O7, 99.5%], sodium chloride [NaCl, 99.5%], [3-(2-aminoethyl) aminopropyl] trimethoxysilane (AEAPTMS) [C8H22N2O3Si, 97%], anhydrous ethyl alcohol [C2H5OH, 99.5%] n-hexane [C6H14, 97%] (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., China), and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide [DMPO, 98%] (Adamas-beta Co., Switzerland) were used in this research. All chemicals were used without further purification in the experiments.

Anhydrous citric acid and AEAPTMS with a mass ratio of 1:20 were mixed in a Teflon-lined reactor under stirring at room temperature for 30 min. The resulting solution was heated to 180 °C for 12 h, and a homogeneous solution can be obtained in yellowish color. The solution was extracted with n-hexane to remove unreacted substances. The residual liquid was removed by rotary evaporation at 60 °C to obtain a gelatinous yellow powder, which is CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pristine NiTiO3 and loading of carbon quantum dots on NiTiO3
ce:section.ce:para: NiTiO3 (NTO) powder was synthesized by modified Pechini method. It was prepared by sol–gel approach and calcination subsequently. In detail, 2.488 g Ni(CH3COO)2·4H2O and 11.53 g C6H8O7 were dissolved in 100 mL C2H5OH under stirring for 30 min until formation of a greenish solution homogeneously. 2.96 mL C12H28O4Ti was blended into the solution dropwise, and it was kept magnetically stirring in ambient condition for an hour. The as-prepared solution was transferred into an oven, and thermally treated at 343 K for 2 days to allow the formation of a gel-like precursor. The gel was calcined at 873 K for 4 h with ramping rate of 5 °C/min and cooling down to ambient condition to receive yellow NTO powder.

CQDs was loaded on NTO by an impregnation-sintering method. In detail, a certain quantity of CQDs solution (1 mg/mL in water) and 200 mg of NTO were mixed by stirring for 15 min and dried in oil bath at 353 K for an hour. The quantity of the CQDs was acquired in the weight percentage to NTO with the values of 1%, 2%, 3%, 4%, 5% and 6%, which are namely C/NTO-1, C/NTO-2, C/NTO-3, C/NTO-4, C/NTO-5 and C/NTO-6, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NTO and CQDs/NTO photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1 mg NbCl5, 15 mL DI water, 15 mL HCl, 0.4 mL C12H28O4Ti and 143 mg NaCl was blended and stirred for 20 min. The as-prepared solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined reactor with stainless-steel autoclave, and a fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) glass (10 mm × 25 mm) was placed into the reactor. The autoclave was kept at 423 K with a ramping rate of 5 °C/min for 8 h to grow Nb doped TiO2 nanorods on FTO glass.

The NTO was coated on the surface of Nb:TiO2 by hydrothermal reaction. Specially, 2.488 g Ni(CH3COO)2·4H2O and 2.96 mL C12H28O4Ti were dissolved in 100 mL C2H5OH. The as-prepared Nb:TiO2 grown electrode was placed into a Teflon-lined reactor with stainless steel autoclave, which was kept at 433 K for 10 h. The prepared photoanodes can be obtained and they were washed with DI water. Subsequently, the as prepared photoanode were calcined at 873 K in the air for 4 h. The CQDs were loaded on NTO photoanode by coating certain quantity of CQD solution on NTO photoanode. Specifically, 1 mg/mL CQDs with the concentrations of 0.047, 0.095, 0.142 and 0.190 mg/cm2 were prepared as the precursor. The solution was coated on NTO photoanode, and dried at 353 K for 2 h.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was carried out in a Hitachi New Generation field emission SEM SU8010 spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed in the Thermo Scientific FEI system. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed in a Niolet iS50 infrared spectrometer, scanning from 4000 to 1000 cm−1. Micromeritics ASAP2020 equipment was used to determine the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas. The sample was degassed at 423 K for 8 h and then analyzed at −77 K. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was studied in a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu-Kα1 radiation (1.54178 Å) in the 2θ ranging from 5 to 80°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were taken on a Thermo ESCALAB250 instrument with a monochromatic A1 Kα line source (200 W) and a charge neutralizer. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) measurement was used to determine the work function, which was carried out with Helium Iα as the ultraviolet source. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–Vis DRS) was obtained in a UV–Vis spectrophotometer in Varian Cary 500 scan system in which BaSO4 was used employed as the reflectance standard. Raman spectra were collected on a LabRAM HR Evolution Raman spectrometer system under 325 nm excitation. The photoluminescence (PL) and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra were obtained in a FLSP920 system with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer was collected in HITACHI UH5300 system with wavelength ranging from 200 nm to 900 nm.

The photocatalytic reactions were conducted in a Pyrex top-irradiation reaction vessel. 50 mg photocatalyst is suspended into 100 mL water, and 1 mM AgNO3 and 0.61 mM La2O3 were added as sacrifice agent and pH buffer agent. The system was evacuated for 5 cycles to eliminate the air prior to illumination. A 300 W Xe lamp (PLS-SXE 300D, Beijing Perfect light Co., Ltd) was used as the light source with a filter to radiate visible light (λ > 420 nm). A water-circulating pump was used to maintain the temperature at 285 K. The evolved O2 was analyzed by an online gas chromatography (SHIMADZU GC 8A) with a thermal conductivity detector and Ar as the carrier gas.

PEC measurements (BAS epsilon Electrochemical system) were conducted with a three-electrode cell, including a Pt cathode, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and NTO based photoanodes. In the system, 0.2 M NaOH solution was used as the electrolyte solution, and an air mass 1.5 globe (AM 1.5G) solar simulator (Newport, USA) was used as the light source. Nyquist plots of the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed under AM 1.5G illumination. Mott-Schottky plots of EIS were conducted at a frequency of 1 kHz in dark, and the Mott-Schottky equation was applied to calculate carrier concentration.

I-EPR analysis was obtained using a Bruker electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (ESP 300E). 20 µL DMPO was blended into 0.5 mL water as a stock, and 2.5 mg photocatalyst was dispersed in the stock solution. 2.5 mg La2O3 and 2.5 mg AgNO3 were suspended in the solution. The suspension was transferred into a glass capillary. The glass was sealed with argon (Ar) gas. DMPO–•OH signal can be obtained by EPR spectrometer under illumination of a 300 W Xe lamp (λ > 420 nm).

The FTIR spectra of D2O adsorbed on NTO and CQDs/NTO were carried out on a NICOLET IS50 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer. 10 mg photocatalyst was loaded into a self-supporting IR disk. The disk was placed into the sample holder and moved into the IR tube. The disk was pumped to vacuum (4 × 10-2 hPa), and thermally treated at 150 ℃ for 2 h to remove water on the surface of sample. Subsequently, 25 μL of D2O was injected into the cell with a syringe. The system was kept for 30 min to reach an equilibrium, and free D2O molecules in the system was removed by pumped for 3 min.

High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) is therefore acquired to study the CQDs on NTO surface. Two different species can be clearly realized as shown in Fig. 1 c and S3. The fringe spaces of 0.27 correspond to the crystal plane of (104), revealing the major composition of the material is NTO. CQDs are presented in amorphous state strongly anchoring on the surface of NTO. In addition, scanning TEM (STEM) is acquired with accompanying energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis to present the distribution of the CQDs on the NTO. High angle annular dark field (HADDF) image of CQDs loaded NTO is presented in Fig. 1 d, and correspondingly, CQDs can be observed in unique size with homogenous distribution on the NTO as presented in Fig. 1 e. We can also observe the elements of Ni, Ti and O, indicating the formation of NTO. Their concentration is further analyzed ( Figure S4 ), and the content of C is only ca. 0.65 wt% and 2.21 at%.

The crystal phase of the sample is further investigated by X-ray diffractometer (XRD) as shown in Figure S5 . All the peaks can be assigned to NTO (JCPDS No. 33–0960), revealing its high purity and crystallinity. The dominant peak at the crystal plane of (104) can be clearly observed at 33.1°, which makes agreement with TEM analysis in Fig. 1 c. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is used to study the chemical composition of the samples. The high-resolution Ti 2p and Ni 2p spectra confirm the formation of NTO. Specifically, the domain peaks at the binding energies of 458.2 and 464.1 eV in Ti 2p spectrum can be assigned as Ti-2p 3/2 and Ti-2p 1/2, respectively ( Figure S6
a). The peaks at the binding energies of 855.8 and 873.3 eV in Ni 2p spectrum are attributed to Ni-2p 3/2 and Ni-2p 1/2, respectively ( Figure S6
b). In C 1 s spectrum ( Figure S6
c), it is worth to note the fact that the peak intensities increase after loading CQDs. In O 1 s spectrum ( Figure S6
d), we can observe the peak at binding energy of ca. 532 eV increase after loading CQDs. This peak is attributed to the CO bond, indicating the CQDs involve O element.

The chemical structure of CQDs is further investigated by FTIR as shown in Fig. 1 i. We cannot observe any signal from pristine NTO. Four domain peaks can be probed after loading CQDs on NTO centering at 1049, 1630, 2920 and 3440 cm−1, which can be assigned to COC, CC/CO, CH, and –OH groups, respectively. These results make agreement with the XPS results, revealing the CQDs are abundant of O elements, and the O site is likely to be active sites for WOR. Raman spectroscopy was also used to study the CQDs ( Figure S7 ). Unfortunately, we cannot detect any D and G bands, which probably owing to limited defects and sp2 carbon atom among the CQDs [35]. Those functional groups at surface of CQDs allow them to act co-catalyst for photocatalytic system. The CQDs become different to the general graphene or carbon nanotube materials since they are normally functionalized for charge transfer for photocatalyst owing to their excellent conductivity [36,37].

Optical properties of the samples are studied by ultraviolet visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV-DRS) ( Figure S8 ). Three major absorption peaks can be observed, revealing NTO is a multi-band semiconductor. This property was confirmed by photoluminescence (PL, Figure S9 ). The main emission peak can be assigned at ca. 440 nm, which corresponds to the absorption of NTO, namely 2.80 eV. Notably in both studies, by increasing the CQDs on NTO, the absorptions of NTO are almost preserved. This observation is owing to the absorption of the CQDs as shown in Figure S10 . The digital photographs of the powder C-NTO are shown in Figure S11 , and it makes agreement with the results from UV-DRS and PL.

To study the activity of photocatalytic WOR, powder based half WOR is first evaluated in a vacuum system using AgNO3 as electron acceptor under Xe lamp illumination (λ > 420 nm), and the performances are presented in Fig. 2
a. The WOR performance gradually increases by increasing the loading concentration of CQDs, and the optimal one can be acquired by C/NTO-4. The rate of WOR of C/NTO-4 is 1.9 µmol/h, which is ca. 7 time to the pristine NTO. A decreased WOR performance is obtained by C/NTO-6, and this phenomenon is probably owing to the light blockage of CQDs on NTO, especially in the UV range ( Figure S10 ). Since CQD also acts as semiconductor, reference experiment is acquired by using CQDs as photocatalyst for WOR. However, O2 gas cannot be detected by an WOR experiment for 2 h, and we can clarify that the improved performance is not owing to the semiconducting property of CQD. The WOR of C/NTO-4 was acquired for 10 h as shown in Fig. 2 b. The preserved performance reveals excellent stability of CQDs and NTO for WOR.

NTO was also demonstrated to coat on Nb doped TiO2 (Nb:TiO2) nanorods as a photoanode, which is further loaded by CQDs for PEC WOR [38], and water splitting reaction can thus be realized by voltage bias. In this case, Nb:TiO2 is a species of conductive material, and the layer forms below the NTO films to facilitate the photoexcitation charge separation and transfer. Despite Nb:TiO2 has presented photocatalytic property ( Figure S12 ), when NTO coats on Nb:TiO2, they cannot be photoexcited since all the UV and visible light would be harvested by NTO. In addition, the carrier mobilities of Nb:TiO2 and NTO are acquired for comparison, and they are calculated from Mott-Schottky plots ( Figure S13 ) of EIS by Mott-Schottky equation [39]. The carrier concentrations of Nb:TiO2 and NTO are 6.81 × 1017 cm−3 and 1.32 × 1017 cm−3, respectively, indicating the Nb:TiO2 layer would be effective to transfer the photoexcitation electrons that injected from NTO. Moreover, the band structure by coating NTO on Nb:TiO2 is investigated as shown in Figure S14
. Heterostructure in Type II alignment can be realized [40], and this alignment would improve separation of photoexcitation charges. Subsequently, CQDs in different density, namely 0.047 to 0.190 mg/cm2, were coated on the as prepared photoanode ( Figure S15 ). It is obvious that the photocurrent density is significantly boosted by loading CQDs as shown in Fig. 2 c. The optimal one is ca. 160 μA/cm2 under illumination of a sunlight simulator by coating 0.142 mg/cm2, which is ca. 6 times to the pristine NTO. When the density of the loaded CQDs is>0.190 mg/cm2, the photocurrent density reduces, and this phenomenon agrees with the powder samples. Nyquist plots of the EIS is recorded ( Figure S16 ) at low frequencies to show the interfacial charge transfer resistance from the photoanode to the electrolyte. The results agree to the photocurrent density, small resistance was received by loading CQDs with the density of 0.142 mg/cm2. The stability of the photoanode is also performed by a 15-hour measurement as shown in Fig. 2 d, and the performance can be preserved. This observation implies all the Nb:TiO2, NTO and CQDs are chemically robust for PEC WOR. The bandgap of NTO is smaller than that of Nb:TiO2. Therefore, the band structures of NTO and Nb:TiO2 can be depicted as shown in Figure S14 .

Subsequently, we have focused on role of CQDs for WOR as it is considered as the main reason for the improved performance of WOR. The chemistry of WOR can be generally understood within four steps, including chemical activation of H2O molecules, the dissociation of HO bond, formation of OO, and O2 releasing [21]. FTIR was acquired to study the chemical adsorption of water on samples. This study is proceeded by D2O in vacuum condition to avoid the influences of water in atmosphere. Once the system achieve vacuum, 25 µL D2O is injected, and the FTIR spectra in this case are close to the pristine D2O ( Figure S19 ) for both C/NTO and NTO. Therefore, we have acquired 30 min after injection of D2O. When the chemical adsorption is balanced, the system is vacuumized again to remove the free water molecules. The resulting FTIR spectra are shown in Fig. 3
a, we can observe the vibration peaks of D-O become to a lower wavenumber, revealing both C/NTO and NTO can chemically adsorbing water molecule. In addition, we can observe that NTO forms broader D-O vibration peaks with more noise signals than C/NTO, indicating that when CQDs is loaded on NTO, its interaction with water significantly increases. Moreover, i-EPR was acquired using same experimental conditions as WOR, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 b. The OH bonding dissociation is considered as the first step during WOR, and this dissociation leads the formation of •OH, which can be trapped by DMPO [41]. With respect to pristine NTO, C/NTO presented increased concentration of •OH as the increased intensity of the peaks in i-EPR, revealing the rate of the •OH production increases when CQDs are loaded by increasing the chemical adsorption of the water on C/NTO surface.

In a conclusion, we had studied the roles of CQDs on NTO as a photocatalytic system for WOR by both powder-based photocatalytic and PEC systems. We had found that the loadings of CQDs effectively boost the WOR performances from both powder-based photocatalytic and PEC systems. The roles of CQDs for WOR were investigated, and it can be witnessed that, on one hand, CQDs play the role to improve the chemical absorption of water molecules. On the other hand, CQDs would promote photoexcitation hole to participate the oxidation process to dissociate the OH bond and formation of •OH. The studies had shown the unique properties of CQDs as co-catalyst for the WOR and improved the understandings of the roles for photoredox catalysis [29,42]. This investigation is potential to stimulate rational design of sustainable carbon materials for WOR and overall water splitting [43], and offer opportunities for advanced redox reactions, such as CO2 reduction, organosynthesis and others [44,45].

Long Wang: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing–original draft. Pingru Cai: . Zuozhen Liu: Formal analysis, Data curation, Visualization. Zailai Xie: Conceptualization, Investigation, Resources, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yuanxing Fang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22075047 and 22072018) and Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province of China (2020 J01446).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.08.196.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. W. Fischer, J. Hemp, J.E. Johnson, Evolution of Oxygenic Photosynthesis, Annu. Rev. Earth Pl. Sc, 44 (2016) 647-683.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Revealing the defect-dominated oxygen evolution activity of hematene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Mohanty, Y. Wei, M. Ghorbani-Asl, A.V. Krasheninnikov, P. Rajput, B.K. Jena, Revealing the defect-dominated oxygen evolution activity of hematene, J. Mater. Chem. A, 8 (2020) 6709-6716.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic Oxygen Evolution from Functional Triazine-Based Polymers with Tunable Band Structures

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoelectrochemical conversion of CO2 into HCOOH using a polymeric carbon nitride photoanode and Cu cathode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Ma, Y. Fang, X. Fu, X. Wang, Photoelectrochemical conversion of CO2 into HCOOH using a polymeric carbon nitride photoanode and Cu cathode, Sustain. Energ. Fuels, 4 (2020) 5812-5817.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient development of Type-II TiO2 heterojunction using electrochemical approach for an enhanced photoelectrochemical water splitting performance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Fang, Y. Ma, X. Wang, Efficient development of Type-II TiO2 heterojunction using electrochemical approach for an enhanced photoelectrochemical water splitting performance, Chinese J. Catal, 39 (2018) 438-445.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Water-oxidation photoanodes using organic light-harvesting materials: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.T. Kirner, R.G. Finke, Water-oxidation photoanodes using organic light-harvesting materials: a review, J. Mater. Chem. A, 5 (2017) 19560-19592.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thickness control in electrophoretic deposition of WO3 nanofiber thin films for solar water splitting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Fang, W.C. Lee, G.E. Canciani, T.C. Draper, Z.F. Al-Bawi, J.S. Bedi, C.C. Perry, Q. Chen, Thickness control in electrophoretic deposition of WO3 nanofiber thin films for solar water splitting, Mater. Sci. Eng. B-Adv, 202 (2015) 39-45.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heterojunction of FeTiO3 Nanodisc and TiO2 Nanoparticle for a Novel Visible Light Photocatalyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.J. Kim, B. Gao, S.Y. Han, M.H. Jung, A.K. Chakraborty, T. Ko, C. Lee, W.I. Lee, Heterojunction of FeTiO3 Nanodisc and TiO2 Nanoparticle for a Novel Visible Light Photocatalyst, J. Phys. Chem. C, 113 (2009) 19179-19184.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coating Polymeric Carbon Nitride Photoanodes on Conductive Y:ZnO Nanorod Arrays for Overall Water Splitting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Fang, Y. Xu, X. Li, Y. Ma, X. Wang, Coating Polymeric Carbon Nitride Photoanodes on Conductive Y:ZnO Nanorod Arrays for Overall Water Splitting, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed, 57 (2018) 9749-9753.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Vertically aligned 2D carbon doped boron nitride nanofilms for photoelectrochemical water oxidation
